turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: We'll hear argument first this morning in Case 12簫8561, Paroline v. United States. Mr. Schneider. APPOINTED BY THIS COURT

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: May it please 簫簫 Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This is a case about statutory construction. Specifically, under 18 USC 2259, must a victim's 簫簫 victim's losses be proximally caused by a defendant's offense conduct before a sentencing judge can award restitution? The short answer to the question is all losses must be proximately caused. Eleven courts of appeals and the government agree that this circuit ignored the plain text of the statute and the enumerated enforcement provisions of 3664 to craft a restitution scheme where the possession of two images of child pornography resulted in the award of $3.4 million to Doyle Paroline which was strictly 簫簫 jointly and severally liable. In my time before the Court, I wish to address, first, why the Fifth Circuit decision is incorrect, and second, why the government's concept of aggregate causation conflicts with its statutory burden of proof contained in 3664(e).

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Well, before 簫簫 before you do that, could you explain why there is an issue of proximate causation here? Why 簫簫 why isn't there clearly proximate cause? Why is it 簫簫 why does it matter?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: Well, I think there has to be 簫簫 in all restitution cases, the issue is whether or not the person's conduct caused the harm and losses, and harm and losses are two different things.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Yes. But why 簫簫 why isn't the issue here a question of factual causation? What losses proximately caused 簫簫 is proximately caused by the possession of child pornography?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: That's a fact簫intensive question, and that's what the district court has to determine, whether on the facts presented, there are identifiable losses that were caused by the defendant's conduct, and there can be.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: It seems you're confusing proximate cause with cause in fact. They are two legal requirements, but not the same. Proximate cause is foreseeability. There's no argument, I think, that anyone who possesses child pornography knows that the individual depicted is going to feel invaded by that viewing, so there's no issue that the person is hurt. The question is a different one, which is the cause in fact. I think that's what my colleague Sam Alito was talking about. So why do you continue to talk about proximate cause when it's a different concept that's at issue?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Is her treatment 簫簫 I think you're arguing that her treatment has to be a "but簫for" this possession.

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: I think they go hand簫in簫hand because when dealing with proximate cause, would the conduct 簫簫 is the losses the natural sequence from the conduct and the result? And what we're dealing with is would the losses have occurred, but for the conduct. And in this situation 簫簫 in this case, where the damage model was created six months before the notice is received, then you don't have the relationship between the loss and the conduct or the notice of the conduct. And it's a real problem. But the 簫簫 the important consideration in interpretation of the statute is the government's burden of proof under 3664(e).

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Why do we 簫簫 why would we 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: Would you agree 簫簫 would you agree that the 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kennedy.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: 簫簫 the losses here were the proximate result at least of the aggregate actions of all the offenders? Would you go that far?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: Justice Kennedy, the problem with aggregate causation does not apply 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: Would 簫簫 would you agree that it is the proximate result of the aggregate actions of all the offenders?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: It would be a harm, but not 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: Pardon me. A proximate harm, right?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: But the problem is when you're dealing with aggregate causation, that conflicts with the statutory burden of proof that the government has, under 3664(e), which is made applicable to the 2059, under Section (b)(2), and you'll find 3664(e) at Appendix 22 to our merits brief. The 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Why 簫簫 why should we get to that question, when we have what seems to be a clear order from Congress, and it is in 2259(a). It says, "The court shall 簫簫 shall order restitution for any offense under this chapter." It sounds like that's a direction that, if there's an offense under this chapter 簫簫 which is undisputed, there is 簫簫 the court shall order restitution.

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: Only if 簫簫 if the losses are the result of the offense because the government's burden of proof is that they must demonstrate, by a preponderance of the evidence, that the losses sustained by the victim are the result of the offense.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Well, it doesn't 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: My question is 簫簫 my question is why isn't it 簫簫 and you were going to point to the statute 簫簫 why isn't it at least the result of the aggregate number 簫簫 aggregate offenders?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: It's on the indictment. Only one person is charged and 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: But he is 簫簫 he is one of the offenders in the aggregate.

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: He's not 簫簫 it's not in the indictment. No one else is in 簫簫 is in front of the sentencing judge. The statute 3664(e) and Section (f) 簫簫 (b)(3)(F), all talk about "the offense," and "the offense" is normally 簫簫 this Court has recognized that, when you talk about "the offense," it's the offense of conviction.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Schneider, on your view, if only one person viewed the pornography, that person would be responsible for the entire damages, but if a thousand people viewed the pornography and the harm was that much greater, nobody would be in 簫簫 would be on the hook for restitution. How could that possibly make sense?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: Justice Kagan, that's not what 簫簫 there are numerous cases where someone who looks at 簫簫 possesses child pornography has been shown to be liable for losses and restitution and some individual cases that it's not. And it's fewer cases than not are, again, no restitution because there's no losses. The losses in 簫簫 it all depends on the facts. In this case, the district judge carefully weighed the evidence. In this case, 簫簫like un 簫簫 unlike any other case, there are competing experts that the judge, district judge, played 簫簫 acted as a gatekeeper of the forensic science and with the challenge to the methodology that was presented by Amy's experts, the judge left no alternative because there was no explanation. And that methodology 簫簫 faulty methodology was part of his decision that he recognized.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Well, what's the answer, though, to the problem, forgetting this case? There's a problem in child pornography cases. Congress clearly wants restitution. Makes sense to me. But if a thousand people look at it, then each one can say, but I didn't cause more than a tiny fraction at most, and so there virtually is no restitution; right? Now, every one of the thousand says that, truthfully, and so therefore the victim gets no restitution 簫簫 opposite of what Congress wanted. But their answer in the lower court was, we'll make each one of those thousand liable for the whole thing. The government's answer is aggregate cause. It's called a concept that they've sort of created, but it's there to answer the problem. What's your answer to the problem?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: It's a 簫簫 it's a very difficult question.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Yeah, it is.

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: And 簫簫 and the answer is to 簫簫 is multifaceted because the government's burden of proof for restitution applies to all restitution statutes. It's the same standard. And you can't carve out one standard of causation for one crime because it's a heinous crime and, as repugnant as child pornography is, the standard of causation has to be the same 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: All right. All right. I suppose that it does. If you have the same problem, you have the same problem. Where there 簫簫 you understand. So how 簫簫 how do we answer this problem? And the difficulty of saying 簫簫 dividing 簫簫 the government doesn't find a thousand 簫簫 you know, it only finds two or three. And so you're going to be 997 who escape. And those three, what should they pay? What 簫簫 should they pay nothing? Should they pay the whole thing? Should we do it somewhere in簫between? How 簫簫 just restating the problem and 簫簫 and you have experience in this field. So what do you really think?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: Well, in some of the cases, there's evidence that 簫簫 of not 簫簫 of the victim having knowledge of an individual, and in that case 簫簫 those cases, there have been awards of restitution, some of them 3,000, 5,000, 15,000, 30,000. But there's some knowledge and there's some evidence for a judge to base a finding of causation to the award of restitution. And it's a fact簫based determination. There has to be some evidence for which the judge makes his determination.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: But your answer is in a large category of cases, and this may be 簫簫 these may be the typical cases, where there's a digital image, and it's out there, and you don't even know that there are a thousand people who've seen it. It's impossible to tell how many people have seen it and certainly impossible to see how many people will see it in the future. It 簫簫 it exists, and it apparently is impossible to eradicate it. Your answer is that, in those cases, the victim gets nothing. I mean, that's your honest answer. And that may be where the statute points, but I think that's your answer to Justice Breyer's question. The victim gets nothing in those cases because it's impossible to determine what percentage of the harm is attributable to this one defendant's possession of the child pornography.

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: I don't 簫簫 and that's not what I'm saying. In the Second Circuit, in Lundquist, was an Amy case where there was a finding of losses and restitution. And the court 簫簫 the Second Circuit specifically addressed how to calculate it. It doesn't have to be precise. We're not saying it has to be a precise determination, but there has to be some evidence for which a judge can base a determination that ties the losses in this case to the conduct. Now 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So what exactly 簫簫 give me an example of what you're talking about. Now, here, you're saying because she didn't know the name of your defendant, you win because you can't say any harm was caused by him because she didn't know his name, correct? Is that your position?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: No, Your Honor. My position is that the district judge made a determination as the gatekeeper of the forensic science that the government didn't prove its case. If you look 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So give me an example of how the government in the hypothetical that Justice Alito spoke about and Justice Kagan, you've got 簫簫 or 簫簫 or Justice Breyer.

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: Lundquist is a perfect example, where there are two reports by their expert explaining how the knowledge of 簫簫 of an individual of Lundquist affected her treatment, affected her day簫to簫day life, and their specific report talks about knowledge of the 簫簫 this individual.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The particular individual? She has to know each individual who 簫簫 who has been viewing her rape on 簫簫 on these pornographic items that have been distributed widely? She has to know each individual, or she can't be covered?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: By 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The woman has undergone serious psychiatric harm because of her knowledge that there are thousands of people out there viewing her rape, but you say she can't 簫簫 why isn't your client at least responsible for some of that? Even without her knowing him, he had two of her pictures. He's one of the thousands of people who viewed 簫簫 who viewed her rape. So why can't she recover some amount from him? I mean, the other side says she ought to be able to recover everything from him. You say she ought to be able to recover nothing. What about something in簫between? What 簫簫 what about figuring out how much of 簫簫 of her total psychiatric damage and all the expenses and what簫not are attributable to this particular person?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: Justice Scalia, the answer to your question is 簫簫 is multifaceted and 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You said that for another question.

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: I understand.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Give me one facet.

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: And Congress dictated that all victims who are identified receive notice, and that notice goes to her lawyer. And in the later cases 簫簫 in the recent cases, she's been notified of every single one of those notices that are received, and appropriate counseling has been given in all the later cases.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Let me ask you. You're mentioning this case. There was another case that also involved and came up at the same time, the Wright case, and there was a settlement there, and it was for 簫簫 what was the amount of money? The amount of damages in the Wright case?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: I think, in Wright, it was $500,000, was the court's order, I believe.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Now, but was there something in the evidence there that made it proper to award restitution in the Wright case, but not in this case?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: They're the same?

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: Yes. I'd like to reserve the remainder of my time.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Dreeben.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: As Justice Breyer indicated, there really are three stark choices before the Court in this case. Paroline's position is that, because of a proximate causation requirement, Amy can obtain nothing in restitution. Amy's position is that, because multiple people have contributed to her single loss, she can obtain the full restitution for all of her losses from every criminal defendant. The government's position is that there is a solution that tort law has developed for providing some restitution for Amy, not the entire restitution that would be owed on account of her entire loss.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Yes. Well, now 簫簫 not because tort law has developed that, I mean, I frankly don't agree with what you say modern tort law is supposed to allow, and 簫簫 and Keeton 簫簫 you know, doesn't really establish that that's what the courts now do. But even without agreeing with that, can't I find that that is the system that this statute intended to impose? I mean, we're dealing here with a question of statutory interpretation. So the question is not what does modern tort law allow, but the question is what, reasonably, could Congress have meant by this statute?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: I agree with that, Justice Scalia.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Because I'm not going to go along with you on 簫簫 you know, modern tort law. I think that's nonsense. I don't think it's true.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: Okay. The 簫簫 the tort law issue is not really all that modern. It dates back to the Restatement (Second) of Torts in Section 443(a). And this Court applied that section in the Burlington Northern case, which was a CERCLA case, to hold that apportionment of the damages was required in a pollution case, where all the pollution had merged into a single 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But how do you square your position with the statutory language? The statutory language requires mandatory restitution order for the, quote, "full amount of the victim's losses." It also forbids courts to 簫簫 to decline to issue an order, double negative, but it can't 簫簫 a court can't refuse to issue an order on the ground that the victim has been compensated by any other source. It seems to me that that language, as crazy as it seems, to you or 簫簫 to you, compels joint and several liability because it's saying any 簫簫 all losses, full amount of losses, and you can't offset her compensation from any other sources.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor, I think that the key to reading the statutory language is that the definition of "victim" refers to a victim of the offense in this case. So the term "victim" means the individual harmed as a result of a commission of a crime under this chapter. The government's burden of proof in Section 3664(e) refers to demonstrating the amount of the loss sustained by a victim as a result of the offense. So it refers to the defendant's offense, not to the collective harm that the 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So why isn't that a but簫for cause? I mean, if you're going to argue that, you have to be arguing but簫for 簫簫

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: I don't think so.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 that their damages have to be the but簫for cause.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: I hope that the Court will read the "as a result of" language to require causation, but to interpret it in light of the way causation has been understood in the unique circumstances that you have here.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, you don't deny but簫for. I haven't understood your submission to be that there need not be but簫for cause here.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: There's collective, aggregate but簫for cause. And the way that tort law dealt with this is not to accept Paroline's position that, even if it 簫簫 you took me out of the equation, Amy's harm would be very severe. Unquestionably true. What tort law said is that, if a multiplicity of actors inflict a single harm and one of them comes forward with a reason that, if accepted, would exonerate all of them, then that cannot be a proper test of causation. The proper test is aggregate causation, where the aggregate group of offenders is the but簫for cause, and each one of the individuals in that set can be regarded as 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: But doesn't 簫簫 doesn't the individual at least cause some harm without regard to the harm caused by the others?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Of course, yes. Now, why isn't it the case 簫簫 why can't the 簫簫 why can't Amy have an expert that comes in and testifies, as a result of their experience, that the psychological harm caused by knowing that those images are available to others, have been viewed by someone else, will require, on average, whatever, a certain amount of counseling for a certain amount of time? So that the one individual is responsible for that harm, regardless of what else happens. Now, he may say, well, it's kind of unfair to stick me with that, but that's a different question than the aggregate causation issue that you're addressing. If you have expert testimony, and there will be expert testimony on the other side, that says it normally requires counseling for someone, when they find out, without regard to anything else, that an individual has viewed this, why doesn't that require 簫簫 or at least allow assessment of the 簫簫 the whole amount?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, that would allow assessment of some of the damages. I 簫簫 I don't think that it would allow assessment of the entire amount because, again, we regard this 簫簫 this statute as enacted against the backdrop of tort law principles that recognized you can have aggregate causation, but it's not appropriate to hold each person in the set responsible for all.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: My 簫簫 I'm sorry. My hypothetical is not aggregate causation. It is that this individual caused this harm.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: If there's a specific amount of harm caused and if Amy could find an expert who would testify to that respect and it would require, say, six counseling sessions, and it would set her back in employment for a year, then I guess a court could, if it found that credible, conclude that a certain amount of loss is attributable directly 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, you're 簫簫 you're confusing whether there's a specific amount of harm caused with whether it is easy to measure that harm. The 簫簫 the tort cases you refer to are cases in which there is a result produced that is not severable. You can't say this person is 簫簫 you know, accounts for this much of it, such as pollution, all the pollution there. The stream is polluted. That's quite different from this case where 簫簫 where this 簫簫 this young woman has been subjected to psychological harm because of thousands of people viewing her rape. Now, this 簫簫 she would be subjected to less psychological harm if it was just one person or ten people or a hundred people. In other words, each person increases the amount of her psychological harm. It is severable the way the tort cases you refer to are not. And so why shouldn't the question in this case be simply he caused a certain incremental amount of psychological harm to 簫簫 to this woman and he should pay for it and it's up to the district court to decide what the proper amount of that payment ought to be.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: Justice Scalia, I think that is consistent with the conclusion that the government reaches, although we take a different route to it 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I understand.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: 簫簫 than Your Honor's position.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Well, is it consistent with human psychology? Are psychologists really able to do this? If it's known that this image has been viewed by 簫簫 even if you could figure out how many people have viewed it, and I think, in the age of the Internet that's impossible, but if you knew that it had been viewed by a thousand people, is a psychologist able to say that her knowledge that it has been viewed by a thousand people causes this much harm? And once she learns that it's now been viewed by a thousand and one people, there's a little bit of additional harm or some increment of additional harm, and we can figure out how much that is. Can a psychologist do that?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: Justice Alito, that is why the government has proposed a different approach to getting to the same conclusion.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Dreeben, I would really like you to tell us, concretely, what the government's approach would be; that is, we have all is one position, nothing is another position, and the middle. The government comes down in the middle, and what I gather from your brief, you say there should be a reasonable formula to arrive at a starting point for further analysis. So I don't know what you propose as a starting point. What is the reasonable formula that the Court should use for its starting point?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg, the courts have approved several different formulas as not being an abuse of discretion. One camp of courts approves a view in which the government submits the number of people who have been ordered to pay restitution to Amy, and that number is then divided into the total harm, measured by monetary means, that Amy has established. And that provides a starting point. So, for example, in this case, that starting point would be around $18,000, and then courts can adjust that.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: How do you get to the 18,000?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: By considering the number of defendants who have been ordered to pay restitution to Amy because that provides some certainty in the face of, as Justice Alito pointed out, the great difficulty in determining how many people have actually viewed these pictures 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: How does that 簫簫 I'm sorry.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: 簫簫 the number of people who have been ordered to pay to restitution represents a judicial finding that, in fact, her picture is involved in that person's offense.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And so it wouldn't 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: How does that work for 簫簫 go ahead.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: It wouldn't include the people who are not prosecuted, and it wouldn't include the people who, in the future, are prosecuted.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: That is correct. And some courts, Justice Ginsburg, have, therefore, required that the formula be adjusted to make some sort of reasonable estimate of the total pool of violators. And so I 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Can I ask you one 簫簫 just one moment. How does that work for the first person?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: It doesn't work very well for the first person, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: And for that reason, I think there are other approaches that do work.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Can we with ease say, look, it's very complicated. I can think of some answers, but they're too complicated for me to tell you what they are. And Justice Scalia has a possible answer. Justice Alito, it seemed to me, is pointing out that the second person to look at the thing, if they're only two, probably adds a lot of damage, but the 1000th person compared to 999 is near zero. Now, do we count this defendant as Number 2 or do we count him as Number 1000? Very hard to answer, no answer, really. But can we say, we can't answer how you do this in practice. Leave it to the sentencing commission. That's their job. Now, can we say that?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: The Court can certainly say that the statute requires reasonable apportionment 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: 簫簫 and the districts courts have jurisdiction to experiment with different formulations.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: But can we say 簫簫 can we suggest that the sentencing commission 簫簫 you can't order them to do it, of course not. But can we suggest that this is the kind of problem, very difficult, technically several ways to do it, that they can go into this. Is that true, that they could go into it and recommend or perhaps order?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: They could recommend a 簫簫 an approach. What the sentencing commission actually did was recommend to Congress that it fix it because the disagreements now pertain to the understanding of the statute. Our submission is that there are a variety of ways to approach this problem. None is perfect.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, could you say more about that? You said that the Chief Justice's question was right, that it doesn't work for the first person, and you said there are better ones. What are the better ones?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: The alternative that I would propose in that instance is to look at other similarly situated child pornography victims and take a look at what district courts have been awarding to those victims in similar types of cases and provide some sort of an average range.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But 簫簫 you know 簫簫 I mean, it struck me, when I read your brief, that it said, I don't know why these courts started where they started, whether each violation is worth 1,000 or 5,000 or 50,000. And it seems as though somebody just plucks an initial number out of the air and then everything is pegged to that initial number, but that the initial number has nothing in particular behind it or am I 簫簫 is it more sensible than that?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: I hope that it's not quite as arbitrary as plucking it out of the air, but the reality is 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But they're certainly not using your theory. Even though it's not plucked out of the air, they're certainly not using your theory.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: On the first one, it's impossible to 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Yes. So they're using some theory.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: They are using some theory.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And you have no idea what that is and can't suggest one, right?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: I think that, sometimes, they articulate various considerations, and other times, they rely on basically a consensus 簫簫 a judicial consensus that in a world in which perfect information and mathematical computations are not going to happen, a reasonable approximation that accords with the judgments of 200 other Federal judges is a sensible way to do this. And I 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: But that makes no 簫簫 I mean, how do you tell the difference 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm not sure what sensible 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: How do you tell 簫簫 your model, how does it work if it turns out that the damages are a million dollars, as opposed to $3.4 million? Is it still $1,200 per offender?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: We would, as I indicated, start with a 簫簫 a division problem and approximate the 簫簫 the number of damages that are allocable to that individual and then adjust it because these defendants are not all identical.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: So you start with 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: So you would expect 簫簫 you would expect the defendant in a case where the restitution sought is a million dollars to pay 50 percent of what he would pay if the damages were $2 million.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: Not necessarily because, as I indicated, Mr. Chief Justice, the defendants aren't all identical. Mr. Paroline possessed two images. Some defendants may possess a hundred images. Some defendants may be distributors, as well as possessors, and courts have taken those factors into account.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: What about taking into account that the perpetrator, the uncle, that the restitution was something like $6,000; right?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: Yes, that's correct, Justice Ginsburg.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: So if there's any kind of proportionality, it seems that the possessor of two images should not be responsible for more than the person who perpetrated 簫簫 perpetrated this horrendous crime.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: Well, his 簫簫 his restitution may have been predicated on a very early period, where it was not clear that Amy would be as victimized as she has turned out to be. The very vice of child pornography possession and distribution is that it continually re簫inflicts the abuse on the victim, so that she cannot make a fresh start and say it's finally over, I've put this behind me, I can now move on with my life. The continuing re簫victimization of her is what justifies and creates the increased harms.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: I would like you to, to the extent that you can, to help district judges get a handle on this, what formula should they use and what adjusting factors should they take into account after they have that starting formula?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg, the 簫簫 the formula that I proposed is the one that the government has been suggesting to district courts around the country. Look at the number of convicted defendants who have been ordered to pay restitution to Amy, divide that into the total harm that the district court finds was the result of the community of child pornography possessors and then adjust it based on the severity of the offense. Courts of appeals have said that other formulations are also not abuses of the district court's discretion, and I would emphasize 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And that's wonderful and 簫簫 you know, I 簫簫 I want to go along with you. This is a bad guy, and 簫簫 and he ought to be punished, and he ought to give restitution, but there is such a thing as due process of law. And what you're saying is 簫簫 I have no idea how much he's going to be required to turn over, whether it's going to be 簫簫 what was it in this case, $3 million?

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: That's the amount of money that Amy has asked for.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I have no idea what he's going to be socked with, and that's not what we usually do with criminal statutes.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: Well, in criminal 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You just can't say leave it up to the district judge 簫簫 you know, and they all come out different ways, but who cares, it's 簫簫 you know, so long as it's within some 簫簫

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: Well, Justice Scalia 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: 簫簫 some reasonable amount.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: Post Booker 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That's just not the way we do law.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: 簫簫 post Booker, that is what happens in criminal sentencing. District judges have an enormous amount of discretion about how much imprisonment time and fine time to use to apply to a defendant. And there is appellate review that helps establish some norms that can look to sentencing guidelines as starting points. But in criminal sentencing, imprecision and estimation is the order of the day. And when you're dealing with 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That's because the statute says so. It says you get from 簫簫 you know, 10 to 20 years, okay? But the statute here doesn't say 簫簫 you know, you'll 簫簫 you'll get between this and this, whatever the district judge likes. It doesn't say that. It says you 簫簫 you should compensate the victim for what 簫簫 for what you've caused her.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: But there are two background principles that I think reinforce that a reasonable estimation is better than all or none. The first background principle is that, in criminal restitution, exactitude is not required. Reasonable estimates are the order of the day. Courts have recognized that. And it's reinforced here by the fact that this restitution provision is kind of a crime/tort hybrid. It's criminal sentencing, but it's enacted against the background of compensatory tort law. And in compensatory tort law, reasonable estimates have long been accepted. In the Burlington Northern case that I referred to earlier, the district court made an effort to do some calculations, and then, at the end, he applied a 50 percent "fudge factor" for estimations that reflected possible calculation errors.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: We have a fudge factor here? Can a 簫簫 can a district judge use what he calls a fudge factor 簫簫 this is a criminal statute. It's not a tort case.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: It is a criminal provision 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: We're going to be liable for a fudge factor.

turns.name: DREEBEN
turns.text: 簫簫 that is 簫簫 that is placed into criminal sentencing with a goal of both compensating victims and determining that each defendant comes face to face with the fact that that person has inflicted some harm on a particular victim. That's a valuable criminal sentencing purpose, and to the extent that the only thing that courts can do in this situation is attempt reasonable approximations, that will not be exact, it's a better solution than saying nothing, as Paroline would do, or saying Amy 簫簫 everything, as Amy would do. That is not proportionate to what the defendant has done, but zero is also grossly disproportionate to what the defendant has done. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Cassell.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Remarkably, this morning, the other parties have taken a statute that promises Amy she will receive restitution for all of her losses and interpreted it to be a statute that has fudge factors, imprecision, estimation, to produce some kind of an award that, in the government's view, is fair. That's not what Congress provided in this particular statute. Congress answered the question presented. You were asking, Justice Breyer, what's the amount? The text of the statute tells us. It is the full amount. Here, Amy has put forward 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Why? I mean, the person didn't cause the full amount. I mean, do you think Congress didn't intend 簫簫 do they want to have people pay for injury they didn't cause?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: We disagree with your premise there, with all respect.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Well, if you think he caused the full amount, fine. But, I mean, immediately, you'll say at least they have four other people that they prosecuted for this, and those people caused it just as much, and so let's divide it by five.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: The solution to the point that you're raising, Justice Breyer, is to recognize that Petitioner was a contributing cause to all of Amy's losses. Amy's losses come from a vast, faceless, anonymous crowd of thousands of people scattered around the globe, from Denver to Denmark, who are looking at pictures of her being raped as an eight簫year簫old girl. And that aggregate group of people all contribute to a loss. And I'm sure that Your Honor is familiar with cases from this Court, for example, the Norfolk & Western case, where asbestos manufacturers all contributed to a particular loss. The result in that case was joint and several liability. Each person who contributed to the loss was on the hook for the damages in their entirety.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: So 簫簫 so Congress assumed that this defendant could be liable, in part, for other people's crimes?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: No, Justice Kennedy. What Congress assumed is that he would contribute to a single loss, and then, at that point, he would be responsible 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: A single law or loss?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: I'm sorry. Loss. He contributed to the loss, and it's important to understand 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: He had two pictures. He did not distribute at all. He was not a distributor, right?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: He was just convicted of possessing two pictures. And 簫簫 and the statute doesn't even require intentional possession, just knowing possession, right? He could have gotten them by accident. He knew he got them, but he never took them off of his computer, but he's still guilty of the crime. So I 簫簫 he's guilty 簫簫 he's guilty of the crime. But to sock him for all of her 簫簫 all of her psychiatric costs and everything else because he had two pictures of her? Congress couldn't have intended that.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Congress did intend that, Your Honor. They said that he is responsible for the full amount of the losses. There's a two簫step process here. First, did this particular defendant caused harm to this particular victim? That's the victim definition found in 2259(c). And there are findings of fact by the district court in this case that the defendant, Petitioner, did harm 簫簫 did harm Amy. And then, at that point, the only question left under this mandatory restitution statute is how much restitution does she get? And Congress has answered that question. She gets the full amount, for five itemized categories of losses, psychological counseling being the most prominent. She's documented, with detailed submissions, that she is going to bear 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Well, but responsible for those as a proximate result of the offense.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: That 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: The only offense shown here in this case is the possession of the two pictures. So how 簫簫 how can all of those amounts be the result of the possession of two pictures?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: The language you have quoted, Mr. Chief Justice, is from Subsection (F). Amy is not seeking any recovery under Subsection (F).

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Well, you understand the statutory principle, that it says, "any other losses suffered by the victim as a proximate result of the offense," which suggests that the prior losses are also limited as a proximate result of the offense.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: No, Mr. Chief Justice, and we would ask you to affirm the Fifth Circuit's En Banc decision, which disagreed with that reading. There are numerous canons of statutory construction 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Why would簫簫 Why would it make sense to say that he's liable for lost income, whether or not it's a proximate result, but when it comes to other losses suffered by the victim, they have to be the proximate result?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: There are a number of answers to that question, Mr. Chief Justice. "Any other losses" is potentially open簫ended. It includes such things as emotional distress damages that could be potentially uncapped. (A) through (E), however, are all out簫of簫pocket pecuniary costs. They are defined. And in this case, we have defined, with precision, what the course of lifetime psychological counseling costs will be for Amy.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Does your case fall if we don't buy your argument, if we read into (F) proximate causation?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: No, Justice Sotomayor 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So why are you wasting time on the argument if you win either way? Tell us without it.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: I think we win either way, and obviously, I have to anticipate that there may be a variety of views on this very difficult question. But let's assume that the Court wants to read proximate cause into other parts of the statute where it does not appear. At that point, the question simply becomes what do we mean by proximate cause? Proximate cause is a legal shorthand for scope of the risk 簫簫 a legal duty. And Congress has defined the legal duty here. For those criminals who commit a serious 10簫year Federal felony, the scope of their risk is they will pay the full amount of the psychological counseling costs 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Can I get back to 簫簫 your point was that all of the (A) through (E) is limited, and you don't need 簫簫 well, it's not. One of the losses that can be gained 簫簫 recovered is lost income. Now, if you take away proximate cause, the argument can be, well, I had to undergo counseling in a particular period, so I wasn't able to get a master's degree in this. Because I didn't get a master's degree, I wasn't able to get a better簫paying job, and therefore, I'm entitled to the income from the better簫paying job that I would have gotten if I had a master's degree, if I didn't have to take the counseling. Now, unless you limit that by proximate cause, it's entirely open簫ended. It's not confined, as you said.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Well, there 簫簫 you're talking about the problem of but簫for causation, then what kind of additional limitation will placed on this.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: No, no. I'm talking about proximate causation.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Right. So there would be an additional cause, which the government argues should be a proximate cause limitation, but there are other limitations that could be attached, and that's what the Fifth Circuit En Banc did. It said, for example, that if Amy suffered a car accident on the way to therapy, that that would not be included. Why? Because, contextually and conventionally, we would not refer to those types of losses as stemming from child pornography. That's proximate cause.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Like Justice Sotomayor and like the government here, I think, even if there is a proximate cause requirement, it has been 簫簫 it has been met, once you adopt the aggregate offense notion. But I wanted to ask you about Subsection (F). My 簫簫 it doesn't seem to me that you 簫簫 whether or not you read the proximate cause element in (F) into (A), (B), (C), (D), and (E), it seems to me what you have to read into (A), (B), (C), (D), and (E) is the portion of (F) which says, "any other losses suffered by the victim as a result of the offense." "Of the offense." Is it only (F) that is limited to the particular offense, and (A), (B), (C), (D), and (E) means those 簫簫 those items that come from anybody else's conviction?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: No. The linkage 簫簫 the particular defendant's conviction comes from Subsection (C). A victim such as Amy has to establish that she's been harmed as a result of this defendant's crime. That establishes 簫簫 there's been a reference to aggregate cause. We think the way to look at this would be 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: And is that another way of saying as a result of this defendant's offense?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: As a result of this defendant's offense 簫簫 we agree that Subsection (C) requires Amy to show that she was victimized by this defendant.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But you 簫簫 you are not claiming, are you 簫簫 or are you? 簫簫 that she's been victimized to the tune of 3.4 million, as a result of this particular defendant's offense?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: We are saying that she 簫簫 that the Petitioner in this case, the defendant, contributed to all of the losses that she suffered.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, contributed to.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But I take it that the point of the government's submission is only contributed to, and now, we have to sort of approximate what the contribution was. And you come in and you say, no, it shouldn't be 簫簫 notwithstanding that this particular offense was responsible for only a part, you think the defendant should be liable for the whole.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: We think that the defendant contributed to all her losses. Let me give you a quick illustration. Next week, when Amy goes to her therapist and is billed $100, the defendant contributed to all $100. And of course, all we have when we get to the additional numbers down the road is simply the fact that she's going to be going to therapy, not just next week 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I guess I'm just not sure what that means, contributed to $100. Does that mean she's 簫簫 the defendant is responsible for the entire $100 簫簫

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: 簫簫 as a matter of fact? Or does it mean that the defendant is responsible as a matter of fact for some lesser part of the entire harm?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: No, the first. He's responsible 簫簫 he is a contributing cause of the entire amount.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I 簫簫 I guess I'm 簫簫 when I say responsible, I don't mean as a legal conclusion. I mean did the defendant cause the entire $100 of harm?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: He contributed to the entire $100, just as multiple polluters contribute to asbestosis 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, that's 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: So he should contribute to the payment. You're saying he contributed to the harm, but he makes 100 percent of the payment. That doesn't seem to me to make much sense.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Well, Justice Scalia, we're pure textualists on this. When you look at the text of the statute, Congress has answered that question. Congress could have written the statute different ways, but they said that she is entitled to recover the full amount of her loss 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, full amount 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: They don't say from that person.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: 簫簫 from the particular offense, Mr. Cassell, so that it's the full amount of whatever the particular offense caused. But you're not saying that this Defendant caused the entire 3.4.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: We're saying he contributed to the entire 3.4 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Well, let me give you this 簫簫

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: 簫簫 along with the other people.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: I'm sorry. I didn't mean to interrupt you.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: No, I 簫簫 you can't disaggregate them, and that's the fundamental problem here. If you look at, for example, our amicus brief with the American professional association involved with the sexual abuse of children, what they say is that you cannot tease out this particular defendant or this particular harm. They all come together to 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: So 簫簫 so contributed, also, a person who didn't have a picture of her, but watched other victims.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Well, no 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: That person contributed to her, too, because it creates a market for the entire situation. So some limiting principle has to come into play, and the limiting principle that I think people are driving at is, because there's a terrible crime, we don't convict the person who didn't do it, all right? And similarly, because this is a terrible crime, you don't require a person to pay what he didn't cause. Now, that's sort of a principle going on. Now, if you accept that, at least hypothetically, can you give any answer 簫簫 I'm afraid you can't and you're going to say 簫簫

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: No, I'm 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: But answer to what seems to be bothering me and a lot of others is, okay, let's accept the notion of cause, but what is a 簫簫 this is called restitution 簫簫

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: 簫簫 it isn't called fines. And so, given that, what 簫簫 how do you do it? How do you distinguish, so she gets some reasonable amount? How do you do it that you're 簫簫 I can think of 簫簫 we've had about four different suggestions here. But what's your opinion, if you lose on the first? What's the best way to do it? That's what I think people are struggling for 簫簫 with.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: The best way to do it is to give Amy the full amount of her losses in this case.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: That's what I suspected you would say.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Well, let me give you 簫簫 Mr. Cassell, let me give you this example. There are pictures like this, and it's known that they are out on the Internet, and so therefore, they are probably always going to be available and who knows how many people are going to view it. And so the first person is caught for possession of some of these pictures and is convicted, and the psychologist testifies that 簫簫 you know, my client 簫簫 that the victim in this case knows that these things are out there, and there's an unknown 簫簫 an unknown quantity of people are going to view it, and my estimate is that the 簫簫 the treatment she is going to need for this is going to be $5 million. All right. Or 3 million 簫簫 $3 million, whatever it is. Now, the first person is convicted, so the first person is assessed restitution in the amount of $3 million, right? But then, shortly after that, ten more people are convicted for possessing the same amount. They all pay nothing because the first person paid everything? That's your answer?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: That's our answer, and that's exactly what Congress wanted. Congress wanted to take these vulnerable victims, of child pornography crimes, Federal sex offense crimes, and domestic violence, and get them full restitution as quickly and effectively as possible. And then, after that, if there's a need to send an additional message to a defendant, he can be fined up to a quarter of a million dollars or sent to prison for a longer period of time.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: What about at least the amount of the judgments she has already received? The total amount, I think, was 3.4 million that you're seeking?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And she has, so far, collected how much?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: As of yesterday, 1,752,000.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Would you at least deduct that from the 簫簫

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Certainly. Absolutely. No, we're not asking for double recovery. Amy simply wants to be made whole. She wants to recover her psychological counseling costs and her losses.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Usually, when we approve joint and several liability, we do so against a background of a functioning system of contribution, where one defendant pays the entire thing, but then can go after 簫簫

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: 簫簫 other defendants and be pretty well assured that he gets their share. Now, here, there's nothing like that, and the question 簫簫 I guess the question is you're asking us to impose that kind of system without the usual thing that goes along with it, which is a sharing mechanism.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: We disagree with the premise here. The background principle for intentional tortfeasors is they got stuck with the entire liability jointly and severally. That was the common law approach. Now, if you want to deviate from the common law approach and read a contribution action into the statute, we're certainly not opposed to this. But the overriding goal of Congress, as it always is when they deal with criminal, intentional tortfeasors, is to pay more attention to the victims and less about some notion of distributing losses among criminally culpable defendants. Congress doesn't give you any indication in this statute that they were concerned that one particular defendant might pay more than his, quote, "fair share," or whatever that means. And the fundamental problem with the government's position is that, after four簫and簫a簫half years of litigation, we still don't have any indication as to how much Amy is going to receive in this particular case. What the government proposes to do is to cast district courts adrift, as they have been for the last four and a half years, to look at a variety of the factors, including, remarkably, if we read the government's brief correctly, one of the factors is the need to get the victim full 簫簫 full recovery.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: In my example, suppose that the first person, who has a restitution order for the full amount, seems unlikely ever to be able to pay that. Does that mean that they 簫簫 you would impose the same 簫簫 keep imposing the same judgment on everybody else, so everybody would be hit with $3 million?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Precisely, until 簫簫 until the victim recovers the full amount.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Well, what about doing 簫簫 what you'd say is make the department, you can find out figures, you could find out from figures what's the likelihood that X number of people will be caught. And say it's 3 out of 1,000. Then what you do is you divide by three. I mean, that way, everybody would be treated fairly, and moreover, the victim would at least have a shot at getting 簫簫

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: But the problem is, in this particular case, the numbers show that at least 70,000 people have viewed Amy's images.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: And how many 簫簫 how many are going to get caught and convicted?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: We have no way of knowing.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Well, you can find out. There's no 簫簫 it's not an impossible question for people to come in and use the numbers and figure it out. My guess would be a very tiny percent.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: A tiny percentage 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: All right. Fine. Then she's going to get a lot of her 簫簫 then she's going to get from each one a pretty good share, only 簫簫

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: What you're assuming, though, is that there's going to be a steady stream of cases, that criminals aren't going to discover new encryption techniques to keep their nefarious activities secrets. We don't know whether there are going to be convictions next year or the year following. And Congress acted against that understanding and said she recovers the full amount from the very first person, and if she gets her compensation, then she's out of the picture. And one of the beauties of our approach is that we estimate, within 2 to 3 years, if you affirm the Fifth Circuit, her restitution requests will be done. If you adopt the government's approach, you could be looking at literally thousands and thousands of cases where Amy will have to 簫簫 will have to be filing piecemeal restitution requests, collecting as little as $47 under some of the calculation theories that the government has proposed, and there's no certainty that she's ever going to get the psychological counseling costs that she desperately needs and the lost income. Now, certainly, Congress could have drafted the statute in different ways, but every single word or every single phrase of this statute at least indicates to you that Congress had one goal and one goal alone in mind, that is, that victims get the full amount of restitution that they desperately need. And we're not talking here about reworking an entire restitution scheme for the broad swath of Federal crime. We're talking about three crimes covered by the Violence Against Women Act, the child pornography cases, Federal sexual assault cases, and domestic violence cases. And it's hardly a surprise to discover, when you look at the Violence Against Women Act, that Congress created a particularly expansive restitution regime for the victims of these particular crimes. And we would direct your attention, for example, to the bipartisan amicus brief of a group of senators who were involved in the drafting of the Violence Against Women Act, and they indicated that their overriding goal here was to make sure that these vulnerable victims 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: This is subsequent legislative history? Is that what this is?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Some of the members of the Court reviewed 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Even those that like legislative history don't like subsequent legislative history.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Well, then, if you want to go the with the textual approach, this is a simple case, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: No, it's not a simple case. You said, rhetorical exaggeration, every word in the statute leads to that. But the key words don't. The key words are "proximate result of the offense." And it's difficult to argue that the entire amount is a proximate result of the offense of two pictures.

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Well, what happened was the Petitioner in this case contributed to the entire amount. And then the way the statute operates is a very conventional way. He becomes jointly and severally liable for the full amount. That's not some kind of innovation. This Court, for example, in the Norfolk & Western case unanimously said that joint and several liability is the traditional approach. And so what Congress has done here is not create some newfangled regime. It has simply taken a joint and several liability regime that this Court has routinely applied against negligent tortfeasors and said, when it comes to criminals who act with scienter, act knowingly or intentionally and cause harm, that they're going to have to make sure that the victims are fully compensated.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: But the point has already been made that it isn't a typical joint tortfeasor situation, where you can seek contribution from people who equally contributed. How would 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: You can please answer.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: How would 簫簫 how would the defendant here go about seeking contribution?

turns.name: CASSELL
turns.text: Well, the defendant would 簫簫 if there was a wealthy defendant, who was unhappy with the share he'd been ordered to pay, he could simply try to find other wealthy defendants out there and interplead them in some kind of a case. We admit that's going to lead to litigation burdens, that Congress wanted those burdens on guilty criminals, rather than innocent victims.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Schneider, five minutes. APPOINTED BY THIS COURT

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: Thank you. This 簫簫 this is a criminal sentencing problem. And Congress, in 1996, apparently codified 簫簫 codified this Court's opinion in Hughey, in creating a burden of proof on the government to demonstrate that the losses sustained by the victim are the result of the offense and created a number of new mandatory restitution statutes that apply, and that doesn't change. So as the reading 簫簫 the plain reading of the statute point (59)簫簫 with the government's burden of proof, requires that the government demonstrate that, for example, the medical services losses of the victim of child pornography are caused by 簫簫 are the result of the defendant's offense. And that's what Congress intended by adopting 簫簫 of 3664(e) and placing it as the enforcement provision of each of the restitution statutes that it created in 1996. It codified Hughey. And what the idea of contribution suggests that convicted sex offenders across the country are going to get together and see who pays what to whom. That's not what Congress intended. This is a sentencing proceeding that requires a particularized, individualized sentence based upon criminal conduct. And that's what 簫簫 what the 簫簫 what Congress intended when it created all these statutes. The ideal solution is to say, Congress, make each defendant pay $1,000 per image. That would be 簫簫 into a fund for restitution for victims. And that 簫簫

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, you're right back to the question Justice Kagan began, that if you have two offenders, that there's going to be recovery. If you have 1,002, there won't be.

turns.name: SCHNEIDER
turns.text: There will always be 簫簫 if there's two offenders that are caught, you may get restitution from one of them. But if 簫簫 but you're looking at what a single judge has to sentence someone. A single judge, like Judge Davis, had one defendant. He didn't have a defendant in Florida or Maine or Oregon to see what that person's background was, doesn't have the benefit of a PSR. He has to look at one person. And in this case, the district court balanced two experts and the methodology that was challenged and simply said, I find she was a victim, I find that she was harmed. But the government didn't meet its statutory burden of proof in this case, and in many other cases, there is losses proved. And there's a difference between harm and loss. In this case, the district court did exactly what the government wanted them to do: Weigh the evidence, listen to the evidence, look at the defendant and see if the government met its burden. And we'd ask the Court to reverse the Fifth Circuit and affirm the district court. Thank you very much.

turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
